plan.reasoning: This is the first attempt (no history). The adsorbate is [CH3], a methyl radical with one carbon atom (index 0) in SP3 hybridization with 1 radical electron. This confirms it is a ReactiveSpecies that binds through its radical carbon. The surface is Os32S64, with available sites including ontop (Os, S), bridge (Os-Os, Os-S), and hollow (Os-Os-S-S, Os-Os-S, Os-S-S). For CH3 radicals on transition metal sulfide surfaces, the most common and typically lowest-energy adsorption is at an ontop metal site (Os), where the radical carbon directly bonds to the metal. This is the standard starting point for methyl radical adsorption. The carbon atom (index 0) has radical_electrons=1, confirming it is the correct binding atom. I will start with ontop Os site, C binding through index 0.

plan.reasoning:
Analysis of history: Two attempts have been made. (1) ontop @ Os gave E=-10.415 eV - a successful adsorption. (2) bridge @ Os-Os gave E=-10.487 eV but with an Unstable Site Warning indicating the adsorbate slipped from the Os-Os bridge to an ontop Os site. This means the Os-Os bridge is thermodynamically unstable for CH3. The current best energy is -10.487 eV (which is actually an ontop Os configuration after slipping). 

Remaining untested sites: ontop @ S, bridge @ Os-S, hollow @ Os-Os-S-S (4-fold), hollow @ Os-Os-S (3-fold), hollow @ Os-S-S (3-fold).

For CH3 radical (SP3 carbon with 1 radical electron, index 0), the carbon is the binding atom. Next logical test: ontop @ S site. S atoms are present on the surface and may offer a different binding environment. After that, if needed, we can try Os-S bridge or hollow sites.

Let me try ontop @ S next, as it is a completely different chemical environment from Os. The CH3 radical binds through C (index 0) to the S atom ontop site. This is a valid ReactiveSpecies plan with adsorbate_binding_indices=[0] and surface_binding_atoms=['S'].
